name: Sundara-Pandya
dates_notes: Pre-Śaṅkara; cited by Sureśvara in the Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi; verses preserved as quotations only.
dating_methodology: Sundara-Pāṇḍya is dated by citation-evidence in Sureśvara and (with much greater uncertainty) in later Śrīvaiṣṇava polemical literature. Terminus ante quem: Sureśvara's *Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* (later eighth century) cites verses attributed to Sundara-Pāṇḍya as authoritative against the *jñāna-karma-samuccaya* opponent. He must therefore antedate the late eighth century; if the verses circulated as authoritative by Sureśvara's time, they probably originated at least a generation earlier. Terminus post quem: the verses presuppose the developed Vedānta-Mīmāṃsā controversy over *jñāna-karma-samuccaya*, which is mature only by the mid-seventh century. The window 600–700 CE follows Hiriyanna (*Outlines*, 1932) and Sastri's introduction to the *Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* (1925); it places Sundara-Pāṇḍya in the immediately pre-Śaṅkara generation. Vedānta Deśika's later identification of a Sundara-Pāṇḍya as a Pāñcarātra-aligned Viśiṣṭādvaita ancestor (in the *Pāñcarātra-Rakṣā*) probably refers to a different figure of the same regional name and is rejected by most modern scholarship as confusion. Note (AI-generated audit): the dating is essentially circular — he is placed in the seventh century *because* he is the kind of pre-Śaṅkara figure Sureśvara cites with approval, and Sureśvara is dated by reference to Śaṅkara. No external attestation, no manuscript, no autograph; the regional name 'Pāṇḍya' suggests Tamil-region provenance but does not by itself date the figure.
core_thesis:
Sundara-Pāṇḍya holds three coupled commitments that are recognizable as proto-Advaita without being identical to the developed Śaṅkara-school doctrine. The first is the essential non-difference of self and Brahman as the settled meaning of the upaniṣadic identity-statements. The second is the structural unfitness of *karma* — bound as it is to action, agency, and the falsely-attributed *kartṛtva* — to be a means to a liberation that consists in the cessation of all such attribution. The third is the sufficiency of correct upaniṣadic apprehension to terminate ignorance. The three theses are dialectically interlocked. If liberation consists in the cessation of false agency-attribution, then *karma*, which presupposes and reinforces *kartṛtva*, cannot produce it; only the cognitive recognition of the non-different self, which dissolves *kartṛtva*-attribution, can. The position is therefore a structural argument against *jñāna-karma-samuccaya*: not the empirical claim that ritual is unnecessary, but the categorial claim that ritual is the wrong kind of cause for the kind of cessation liberation requires.

The argument is sharper than the bare statement of immediacy-of-knowledge that Śaṅkara and Sureśvara will systematize. Sundara-Pāṇḍya is not arguing that ritual is harmful or to be abandoned; on the cited verses, his position is consistent with continued ritual observance as preparatory or as part of the social-religious frame within which upaniṣadic study takes place. What he denies is that *karma* contributes to the production of liberating cognition. The cognition is not produced; it is recognized. The denial is structural, not behavioral. This distinction matters because it is exactly what Brahmadatta's *jñāna-karma-samuccaya* contests: Brahmadatta holds that liberating cognition is itself the kind of cognitive event whose generation requires *bhāvanā*-cultivation, and *bhāvanā*-cultivation is a species of enjoined activity. Sundara-Pāṇḍya's verses, on Sureśvara's reading, refuse this assimilation: the recognition of non-difference is not produced by activity, because it is the cessation of the activity-frame as such.

The philosophical force of the position is dialectical. Sundara-Pāṇḍya supplies Sureśvara with a pre-Śaṅkara authority for the immediacy-of-knowledge thesis, against the *bhāvanā*-school of Brahmadatta and Maṇḍana. The fact that Sureśvara needs such an authority is itself evidence that the immediacy-thesis was contested in the immediately pre-Śaṅkara generation. Sundara-Pāṇḍya's verses do not articulate the developed *māyā*-doctrine, the *adhyāsa-bhāṣya* analysis of superimposition, or the doctrine of *sākṣi-caitanya* that distinguishes mature Advaita; they articulate the more basic non-difference / immediacy / *karma*-inertness cluster. He is therefore proto-Advaitin in the precise sense that the structural commitments of the position are present without the developed metaphysics. Whether he was, in his own self-understanding, a member of an identifiable school is not preserved; whether he was Tamil-region, as his name suggests, and whether he is to be identified with any other named figure of the same region, are unsettled questions that the citation-evidence cannot resolve.

What the citation does not preserve is Sundara-Pāṇḍya's positive account of how the recognition of non-difference operates as a cognitive event. Sureśvara's own answer — direct upaniṣadic *anubhava* generated by the *mahāvākya* — may or may not be Sundara-Pāṇḍya's; the verses preserved by Sureśvara give the negative theses (against *jñāna-karma-samuccaya*, against *karma* as cause of liberation) more sharply than the positive epistemology. The conservative claim is that Sundara-Pāṇḍya held the structural commitments that distinguish the immediacy-school from the *bhāvanā*-school, and that Sureśvara's appeal to him is philologically warranted by the existence of an actual textual source whose contents we recover only through the citations.

engaged_works.title: Lost verses cited in Naishkarmya-Siddhi
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Verse-corpus is lost; preserved only as citations within Sureśvara's Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi.
engaged_works.summary: Sundara-Pāṇḍya's literary corpus is lost. What survives is a set of verses (the most-cited cluster appears in *Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* book 1, where Sureśvara invokes them as authoritative against the *jñāna-karma-samuccaya* opponent) which Sureśvara introduces with explicit attribution and treats as authoritative on the side of immediate *jñāna* as the direct cause of *mokṣa*. The verses articulate three coupled theses. First, the essential non-difference of self and Brahman as the settled meaning of the upaniṣadic identity-statements. Second, the structural unfitness of *karma* to be a means to a liberation consisting in the cessation of agency-attribution: *karma* presupposes the *kartṛtva* whose cancellation is the very content of liberation, so *karma* cannot produce liberation. Third, the sufficiency of correct upaniṣadic apprehension to terminate ignorance, with the negative implication that no quantity of additional ritual cultivation contributes to that termination. The position is recognizably proto-Advaita: the structural commitments of the immediacy-school are present without the developed *māyā*, *adhyāsa*, or *sākṣi-caitanya* doctrines that mature Advaita systematizes. Sureśvara's appeal to Sundara-Pāṇḍya is the principal evidence that the immediacy-thesis was contested in the immediately pre-Śaṅkara generation and that Sureśvara needed a pre-Śaṅkara authority to ground the position against the *bhāvanā*-school of Brahmadatta and Maṇḍana. Vedānta Deśika's later mention of a Sundara-Pāṇḍya in the *Pāñcarātra-Rakṣā* probably refers to a different figure of the same regional name and is generally rejected as the same author by modern scholarship (Hiriyanna, *Outlines*, 1932; Sastri, *Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi*, 1925, introduction). Nothing of his original work, its title, or its full extent can be recovered.
